title: Open-RAG-Eval: Reference-Free RAG Evaluation (UMBRELA, AutoNuggetizer, Hallucination, Citation)
one_liner: Score a RAG pipeline's retrieval relevance, answer completeness, factual consistency, and citation accuracy without golden answers, using Vectara's open-rag-eval TREC-style evaluator with an LLM judge.
problem_solved: Teams cannot tell whether their RAG system is actually good, or which of two configurations is better, because building golden question-answer datasets is slow and expensive. Open-RAG-Eval evaluates RAG outputs reference-free: it needs only the queries, the retrieved passages, and the generated answers, then applies research-backed automated metrics (UMBRELA per-passage relevance grading, AutoNuggetizer answer-completeness scoring, HHEM-based hallucination detection, and citation precision/recall/F1) to quantify retrieval and generation quality at scale.
failure_modes: I have no ground-truth answers, so I can't measure whether my RAG system works
I changed my chunking/retriever/prompt and can't tell if the new configuration is better or worse
My retriever returns passages but I don't know which ones are actually relevant to the query
Answers sound plausible but may be hallucinated relative to the retrieved passages
The LLM cites source [1] for a claim but I don't know if that passage really supports it
Generated answers may be missing key facts that were present in the retrieved passages
Manual spot-checking of RAG outputs doesn't scale and isn't reproducible
when_to_use: You need to compare two or more RAG configurations (chunk size, retriever, reranker, prompt, model) and have no labeled dataset
You can export your pipeline's per-query outputs (query, retrieved chunks, generated answer) to a CSV or connect via a supported connector (e.g. Vectara)
Your generated answers include inline citations like [1], [2] and you want to verify citation faithfulness
You want separate diagnostics for retrieval quality (UMBRELA per-passage 0-3 relevance) versus generation quality (nugget coverage, hallucination, citations), so you know which stage to fix
You want a repeatable regression-test harness for RAG quality as part of CI or periodic benchmarking
You need per-query, per-passage intermediate scores for debugging, not just one aggregate number
tradeoffs: Requires an LLM judge (GPT-4o-mini in the notebook), so each evaluated query costs several judge calls; large eval sets incur real API cost and time (~5s/query observed for 10 queries)

Reference-free metrics are proxies: UMBRELA and AutoNuggetizer labels come from an LLM judge and inherit its biases and instability; they measure consistency with retrieved passages, not real-world correctness
Hallucination scoring downloads and runs Vectara's HHEM model locally (transformers-based), adding a dependency and a 512-token limit warning on long passages

Evaluation data must be shaped into the framework's expected format: one row per retrieved passage, per-query generated_answer, passage_ids matching citation markers; adapting a custom pipeline requires writing a connector or CSV exporter

Not a fit if you already have golden answers and want exact-match/semantic-similarity scoring against references; classic reference-based eval (e.g. RAGAS-style with ground truth, DeepEval) may be more direct
The citation metric only helps if your generation step actually emits structured citations
This notebook evaluates offline pre-collected outputs; it does not build or run a RAG pipeline itself
how_it_works: The notebook demonstrates Vectara's open-rag-eval framework on pre-computed RAG outputs for the FiQA (financial Q&A) subset of the BEIR benchmark. RAG outputs are stored in a CSV with one row per retrieved passage (columns: query_id, query, passage_id, passage, generated_answer, where the answer appears on the first row of each query group). A CSVConnector.fetch_data() loads these into RAG result objects. A TRECEvaluator is constructed with an OpenAIModel judge (gpt-4o-mini) and evaluator.evaluate_batch(rag_results) scores every query. Four metric families are attached to each scored result: (1) UMBRELA assigns each retrieved passage a relevance grade 0-3 (NO_RELEVANCE / RELATED / PARTIAL_ANSWER / EXACT_ANSWER) against the query — a retrieval-quality metric; (2) AutoNuggetizer extracts atomic information 'nuggets' a good answer should contain from the passages, labels each vital/okay, then checks whether the generated answer supports / partially supports / does not support each nugget — an answer-completeness metric; (3) a Hallucination score in [0,1] from Vectara's HHEM model measures factual consistency of the answer with the retrieved passages (threshold ~0.5); (4) a Citation metric checks whether each cited passage actually supports the sentence citing it, aggregated into weighted precision/recall/F1. Results are saved via eval_scores.to_csv and summarized with TRECEvaluator.plot_metrics into a comparison chart (multiple result CSVs can be overlaid to compare configurations). Although evaluation is the stage, its metrics diagnose both the retrieval and generation stages separately.
key_dependencies: open-rag-eval (Vectara): TRECEvaluator, OpenAIModel judge, CSVConnector, eval_scores
Vectara HHEM model (loaded internally via transformers for hallucination scoring)
related_techniques: end-2-end_rag_evaluation: alternative (custom completeness/relevance/hallucination metrics vs this packaged framework)
deep_evaluation / DeepEval-based evaluation: alternative reference-based evaluation approach
simple_rag: prerequisite (you need a working RAG pipeline producing query/passages/answer triples to evaluate)
reranking: downstream beneficiary (UMBRELA per-passage scores reveal whether reranking is needed and whether it helped)
graph_rag_local_attribution: complements (citation metric here verifies attribution quality that attribution-focused pipelines produce)
self_rag / crag: complements (evaluation identifies retrieval/grounding failures these corrective architectures try to fix at runtime)
summary: Demonstrates Vectara's open-rag-eval framework for evaluating RAG systems without golden reference answers. The input is a CSV of pre-collected pipeline outputs (query, retrieved passages one-per-row, generated answer with citations) from the FiQA/BEIR financial Q&A dataset, loaded through a CSVConnector. A TRECEvaluator driven by an LLM judge (GPT-4o-mini) runs evaluate_batch and attaches four metric families per query: UMBRELA grades each retrieved passage 0-3 for relevance to the query (diagnoses retrieval failures); AutoNuggetizer extracts vital/okay information nuggets from passages and checks whether the generated answer supports them (diagnoses incomplete answers); a HHEM-based hallucination score in [0,1] measures whether the answer is factually consistent with the retrieved passages; and a citation metric computes precision/recall/F1 of whether cited passages actually support the citing sentences. Results export to CSV and plot_metrics overlays multiple runs for A/B comparison of RAG configurations. Use it to regression-test or compare chunking/retrieval/generation choices when building labeled eval sets is impractical; costs are judge-LLM API calls and format adaptation of your pipeline outputs.
notes_for_chunking: Well-structured, small notebook (~19 cells) with clean markdown headers that map one-to-one onto metric families: Overview, RAG Evaluation outputs, Run an Open-RAG-Eval Evaluation, Review Evaluation Results (with H3 subsections UMBRELA Metric, AutoNuggetizer Metric, Hallucination Metric, Citation Metric), Plotting results. Markdown/code ratio is high (~50/50) and each H3 metric section is self-contained (explanation cell + inspection code cell + printed output), making header-based section chunks natural. No pip-install cells to strip. Junk to strip: long pretty-printed passage/answer outputs in the UMBRELA and AutoNuggetizer cells (hundreds of lines of FiQA passage text), tqdm progress-bar output, HHEM tokenizer warnings, and a base64 PNG output in the final matplotlib display cell. Caveat for extraction: the AutoNuggetizer output cell shows nuggets about an unrelated query because the code reads scores from scored_results[0] while iterating to find a different query — do not treat that printed output as a faithful example of the metric. The Overview cell carries most of the intent/context and should anchor any summary chunk.
